page.title: How to Hire a Chief Revenue Officer (CRO) in the UK
page.intro:
The Chief Revenue Officer role has emerged as one of the most critical executive appointments for growth-stage and scale-up businesses. Distinct from a Sales Director, the CRO owns all revenue-generating functions — sales, marketing, customer success — and is accountable for the complete revenue engine. Finding someone who can genuinely think cross-functionally across these disciplines is the central challenge.

The CRO role originated in SaaS and technology businesses, where the alignment of sales, marketing, and customer success around a common revenue metric (ARR, NRR) is structurally important. It has since spread to professional services, financial services, and other sectors where the commercial model is sufficiently complex to warrant integrated revenue leadership.

A key distinction is between a CRO who inherits established functions and a CRO who must build them. The profile of someone who can scale a £2m ARR business to £20m is different from someone who can optimise a £100m revenue engine. The brief must be specific about the stage and the mandate.
considerations: Define whether you need a CRO or a senior Sales Director — the roles are structurally different and the profiles do not overlap at senior levels.
Clarify which functions report to the CRO — a CRO without ownership of marketing cannot drive the pipeline alignment the role requires.
Be specific about revenue targets, growth expectations, and the go-to-market model — a CRO brief without commercial context produces a weak shortlist.
Assess unit economics and CAC/LTV metrics before briefing — the best CROs will ask these questions in their first conversation.
Ensure the CRO has a genuine seat at the executive table — a CRO who cannot influence product and pricing decisions is structurally hampered.
page.timeline: 10–14 weeks from briefing to accepted offer
page.whySearch: True CROs — executives who can own sales, marketing, and customer success simultaneously with a data-driven, systems approach — are rare. Most candidates are strong in one function but lack cross-functional breadth. A headhunter who specialises in commercial leadership appointments can identify the profiles that genuinely span these disciplines and approach them before they reach the open market.
